output_summary: profiled datasets: dbpedia, metacritic, sales.
This run produced an initial draft of the pipeline.

The pipeline was created to load three XML game datasets named dbpedia, metacritic, and sales, align schemas with an LLM-based schema matcher using dbpedia as the reference schema, and rename columns in the other two datasets accordingly.
The pipeline added lightweight normalization fields including name_norm, releaseYear_norm, criticScore_num, userScore_num, and globalSales_num to support comparison across sources.
The pipeline introduced pairwise blocking for dbpedia-metacritic, dbpedia-sales, and metacritic-sales using StandardBlocker on the normalized game name field name_norm.

The pipeline introduced rule-based entity matching for all three dataset pairs using Jaccard string comparators on normalized name, developer, and platform plus a numeric comparator on normalized release year, with weights of 0.55, 0.2, 0.15, and 0.1 and a threshold of 0.75.

The pipeline concatenated all pairwise correspondences and added a rule-based fusion strategy with longest_string for name, releaseYear, developer, platform, series, ESRB, and publisher, and maximum for criticScore, userScore, and globalSales.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run created an initial draft of the evaluation script for the fused output.
The run aligned fused records to gold IDs through an evaluation view derived from _fusion_sources and then evaluated fields with type-specific matching functions registered in a DataFusionStrategy.
The strategy used tokenized matching for text-like entity fields, year-only matching for releaseYear, numeric tolerance matching for score fields, and set equality matching for list-like fields.
The fields name, developer, platform, publisher, and ESRB were grouped under tokenized_match.
The field releaseYear was evaluated with year_only_match.
The fields criticScore and userScore were grouped under numeric_tolerance_match.
The field genres_genre and any additional detected list-like non-underscore-prefixed columns were grouped under set_equality_match after normalization.
The run added _parse_source_ids to parse serialized source ID collections from _fusion_sources using json.loads and ast.literal_eval.
The run added _detect_gold_prefix to infer the dominant gold ID prefix and use it during candidate eval_id selection.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 61.34%; evaluation attempt counter is 1.
This run was an evaluation refinement of the existing pipeline.
The pipeline replaced lightweight normalization with broader helper-based normalization for names, platforms, companies, years, and numeric fields.
Blocking was tightened from using only name_norm to using both name_norm and releaseYear_num across all dataset pairs.
Matching shifted to comparators on name_norm, exact releaseYear_num, platform_norm, and developer_norm with weights changed to [0.6, 0.15, 0.15, 0.1] and the threshold raised from 0.75 to 0.82.

Fusion changed several attribute fusers, including releaseYear to prefer_non_null, developer and publisher to most_common_string, platform to best_platform, criticScore to best_critic_score, userScore to best_user_score, ESRB to most_common_string, and globalSales to max_numeric_as_string.
Entity resolution became more conservative by requiring stronger agreement on normalized title and exact release year instead of relying on title-only blocking with a one-year tolerance.
Fusion behavior shifted away from longest-string and raw maximum heuristics toward consensus-based and domain-aware fusers for categorical and score attributes.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or a repair.
The dominant strategy kept tokenized matching for text fields, year-only matching for releaseYear, and numeric tolerance matching for criticScore and userScore.
The refinement changed the default handling for detected list-like columns from set equality matching to tokenized matching.
Name, developer, publisher, platform, and ESRB were evaluated with tokenized matching as a text-oriented field group.
ReleaseYear was evaluated separately with year-only matching as a date-specific field.
CriticScore and userScore were evaluated as numeric fields with numeric tolerance matching.
Auto-detected list-like columns that were not already assigned explicit functions were grouped under tokenized matching in the revised logic, replacing the previous set-equality treatment.
The run retained the eval-view construction that maps fused records to gold IDs through parsed fusion sources, gold-prefix detection, confidence-based sorting, and eval_id deduplication.
List-like column detection and normalization across fused and gold data were preserved to support fair comparison before evaluation.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 84.87%; previous logged accuracy was 61.34%; evaluation attempt counter is 2.
This run was a refinement of the existing evaluation pipeline.
The blocking strategy changed from using both normalized name and exact release year to using a new title-only blocking key called name_block.
The matcher relaxed release year comparison from an exact match with max_difference 0 to a near match with max_difference 1 while keeping title, platform, and developer comparators.
Fusion changed developer and publisher from most_common_string to canonical_from_norm with normalize_company, and changed ESRB from most_common_string to a normalized canonical_from_norm-based fuser.
The normalization layer was expanded with stronger text cleanup, richer title normalization, a blocking_name helper, broader platform mappings, ESRB normalization, and releaseYear_clean generation.
The run shifted matching behavior away from strict title-plus-year blocking toward title-centered blocking with a slightly more tolerant year match.
The run shifted fusion behavior from raw majority string selection toward normalization-aware canonical value selection for company fields and ESRB values.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or repair.

The dominant strategy kept field-specific evaluation functions and refined them by using tokenized matching for text fields, year-only matching for releaseYear, numeric tolerance matching for score and sales fields, and a new exact case-insensitive matcher for ESRB.
Name, developer, publisher, platform, and series were grouped under tokenized matching behavior.
ReleaseYear was handled as a dedicated year-only comparison field.
CriticScore, userScore, and globalSales were grouped under numeric tolerance comparison behavior.
ESRB was separated from generic tokenized text fields and evaluated with exact case-insensitive matching.
The run added an exact_match_case_insensitive helper to tighten ESRB evaluation semantics.
The run added registrations for globalSales with numeric_tolerance_match and series with tokenized_match.
The list-normalization import fallback was made safer by guarding __file__ access and using Path composition consistently.
The evaluation logging was simplified through compact_metric_view to print a smaller structured summary.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 59.66%; previous logged accuracy was 84.87%; evaluation attempt counter is 3.

attribute_fusers.name: longest_string
attribute_fusers.ESRB: lambda values: canonical_from_norm([normalize_esrb(v) for v in values if pd.notna(v)])
